html:
Built-Up Roof Systems on Spartanburg's Legacy Industrial Buildings

Built-up roofing, alternating plies of asphalt-saturated felt with bitumen between each layer, is still what we find under the surface on a lot of Spartanburg County's oldest industrial stock. Textile mill buildings around Glendale, Clifton, and Arcadia, several with sawtooth or monitor roof configurations from their original construction, carried BUR for decades before any single-ply system existed as an alternative.

Where BUR Still Makes Sense

We still spec new built-up systems on select projects, mainly larger low-slope roofs with heavy, constant rooftop equipment traffic where the multi-ply redundancy and gravel ballast offer physical protection that a thinner single-ply membrane does not match as well. Older sawtooth mill roofs undergoing renovation into flex or light industrial space sometimes stay with BUR for consistency with the existing structure and drainage design, rather than converting to a different system entirely.

Ply Count and Bitumen Selection

Ply count on a built-up roof typically runs three or four, and we specify that count based on the roof's exposure, expected traffic, and the manufacturer warranty tier the owner wants. Asphalt bitumen is standard in this market; coal tar systems are rare on the buildings we work on now, though we still encounter them occasionally on some of the oldest mill structures during a tear-off.

Application can be hot-mopped or cold-process depending on job-site conditions, access, and any restrictions the owner or jurisdiction places on hot work over an occupied building.

Surfacing: Gravel, Cap Sheet, or Coating

Traditional BUR surfacing uses embedded gravel ballast, which adds UV protection, fire resistance, and a durable wear layer, but also adds significant dead load that has to be confirmed against the structure's capacity, particularly on older mill decks. A granulated cap sheet surface is a lighter alternative that still gives UV and traffic protection without the ballast weight. On roofs where the built-up system is structurally sound but the surfacing has degraded, a silicone or acrylic coating can extend service life without a full tear-off. We assess granule loss, felt exposure, and general wear pattern across the field before deciding whether resurfacing or a full coating system is the more cost-effective path for that specific roof.

  • Deck and structural load evaluation, particularly on older mill buildings
  • Ply count and bitumen type selection matched to exposure and traffic
  • Base and interply felt installation with correct overlap and mopping rate
  • Surfacing selection: gravel ballast, cap sheet, or protective coating
  • Flashing at monitors, curbs, and parapet walls common on sawtooth roofs
  • Drainage verification, including tapered insulation where ponding is a concern

Drainage on Older Low-Slope Roofs

A meaningful share of the built-up roofs we assess in this county have drainage problems that predate any membrane issue: original slope that was never adequate, drains that were relocated or blocked during past renovations, or ponding areas that have gotten worse as the deck has settled over decades. We look at drainage as a structural and design question first, since resurfacing a roof without correcting standing water just means the new surface fails on the same schedule as the old one. Correcting drainage on a BUR roof often means adding tapered insulation crickets at problem areas rather than reworking the entire field, which keeps the cost of a fix proportional to the actual size of the ponding problem.

Repair, Recover, or Full Tear-Off

A built-up roof with sound plies underneath a failed surfacing layer is frequently a recover or coating candidate rather than a full tear-off. We confirm ply condition and moisture content with core cuts before recommending which path fits, since gravel-surfaced BUR roofs can hide moisture problems under the ballast that are not visible from a walk-through alone.

Structural Considerations on Mill-Building Conversions

Buildings originally built for textile production often used heavy timber or early steel truss framing designed for a different loading scenario than what a modern reroof or added rooftop equipment introduces. Before recommending a gravel-ballasted system on one of these conversions, we confirm the structural capacity with whatever documentation is available, and we recommend an engineer's review where that documentation does not exist or the building's history is uncertain.

This step matters more on older mill sites than on new construction, since a miscalculation on a hundred-year-old truss system carries consequences a new steel-frame warehouse simply does not present.

Common Questions on Built-Up Roofing

Is built-up roofing still a good option for a new industrial building?

It can be on roofs with heavy equipment traffic where the multi-ply redundancy adds value, though single-ply systems are more common on new construction in this market overall.

Can gravel ballast be replaced with a lighter surfacing option?

Yes, provided the underlying plies are sound. We can convert to a cap sheet or coated surface during a recover to reduce dead load on older structures.

How do you check for moisture under a gravel-ballasted BUR roof?

We use core cuts and moisture scanning to assess the insulation and felt plies before recommending recover, coating, or tear-off.

Do sawtooth or monitor roof configurations complicate a BUR reroof?

They add complexity at the flashing and drainage details, which is why we spend extra time evaluating those transitions on mill-building roofs before finalizing a bid.

Can a coating extend the life of an aging built-up roof?

In many cases yes, if the plies are structurally sound and moisture-free. We confirm that with testing before recommending a coating over a full replacement.
